title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] TYLER CROWE: Hi,
all, I'm Tyler Crowe, a senior product manager at
Firebase and Google Cloud. MICHAEL BLEIGH: And I'm
Michael Bleigh, an engineering lead on the Firebase team. TYLER CROWE: Today, we'll
walk through a movie review app that was built on
Firebase Data Connect. Then we'll add an agent live. So about a month
ago, Michael and I were thinking about what we
would make for our I/O demo. We came up with the idea
for a movie review app and decided to go to Gemini
to help us get started. MICHAEL BLEIGH: Thanks, Tyler. Data Connect
integrates with Gemini to help you go from idea
to full app data model with just a prompt. So that's where I started
in just a few seconds, I have a great start for our
app with tables already defined. If I want to make changes, I can
ask Gemini to do the updates. Once I'm ready, I
can click Deploy, and Firebase will provision
a Cloud SQL Postgres database and populate it with
the schema for my app. But let's jump forward
in time a little bit and take a quick tour of the
app that we've been developing. The Home page shows new
releases, top-rated movies, and a selection
of recent reviews. Each movie shows
its average rating. I can also browse the
selection of movies with a rich filter
interface, allowing me to select the rating,
genres, and release years I'm interested in as the
data is quickly filtered to reflect my selections. If I visit a movie's detail
page, I can see the cast and reviews, I can also log
my own watch of the movie. Let's say I watched this
movie last night at home and give it a quick review. My review will show up
on the movie's page, as well as in my
personal Watch History. Finally, I can
search for movies. Our app search is powered by
Data Connect's integration with Vertex AI Text embeddings. When I search, it will find
semantically relevant movies, not just close text matches. Here, for instance, when I
search for "mythical creatures," the top results are all
highly relevant for my search,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
with Vertex AI Text embeddings. When I search, it will find
semantically relevant movies, not just close text matches. Here, for instance, when I
search for "mythical creatures," the top results are all
highly relevant for my search, even though none of them have
mythical creatures in the title. As you can see, my app is off to
a great start with many features already implemented. Let's learn a little bit more
about how Data Connect is powering our app. Back to you, Tyler. [CLICKING] TYLER CROWE: So now
that we've seen the app, let's see where it
gets its data from. Firebase Data Connect
brings the ease of use and rapid development
of web and mobile apps from Firebase, matched
with a relational database offering with schema
and powerful querying. I'm happy to share that
Data Connect is now generally available. We've added new features like
Gemini AI assistance, where you can tell Gemini
your app idea and it will generate
your schema, queries, and mutations needed
to build your app. We've expanded the query
capabilities of the service, as well as support for popular
frameworks React and Angular. So what does Data
Connect do for you when you want to
build your next app? We built Data Connect
on a single principle. You write the query,
we do the rest. When you craft a
query on your schema, you get a secure
API endpoint that has built-in authentication
and authorization policy. We generate typesafe SDKs for
Android, iOS, Flutter, and web, and in addition, we'll
generate a tool definition for function calling of
that query from Agents. We start with our schema
and define our query. In our query, you
can see that we have security built in
with the auth directive, and are pulling movies
within a certain date range. Data Connect will take
the schema and query and generate SQL to maintain
your schema inside your Cloud SQL instance. We define a secure API
endpoint that can be called, and we define
typesafe SDKs that can be called with a single
line from your client. Data Connect accelerates
your app development
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
and generate SQL to maintain
your schema inside your Cloud SQL instance. We define a secure API
endpoint that can be called, and we define
typesafe SDKs that can be called with a single
line from your client. Data Connect accelerates
your app development by abstracting the
infrastructure away that you need to manage to run your app. It connects Cloud
SQL to your app, and so you simply need to
define the queries and mutations from your app with the
integrated authentication and authorization policies. Data Connect translates
the GraphQL into SQL to read and write data to
that Cloud SQL instance. Data Connect even handles
embedding generation from Vertex AI to enable
semantic search in your apps. That was the search that looked
through the movie catalog in the demo, based on the
meaning of the search, instead of the
exact search word. So how does semantic
search work? A vector embedding represents
the meaning of an object as the AI understands it in
a multi-dimensional space. So let's break down
what that means. In my example here, I have
a two-dimensional space that already has some embeddings
generated for two places. One is a mountain, and
the other is a beach. If I search for snow
activities, the embedding will fall closer
to the mountain, so the search will return the
data in my database associated with the mountain. If I search for
surfing, the AI knows that surfing is
related to the beach, so it will return the data
associated with the beach embedding from my database. Data Connect makes
it really easy to create these vector
embeddings with Vertex AI and store them in Cloud SQL. With a single line
in a mutation, Data Connect will generate
the vector embedding and write the data
into Data Connect. You write the mutation, and
Data Connect handles the rest. The same goes for semantic
search with vector embeddings. When the user enters
a search term, Data Connect will generate
an embedding from Vertex AI and then execute a
similarity search for related data within Cloud SQL.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
The same goes for semantic
search with vector embeddings. When the user enters
a search term, Data Connect will generate
an embedding from Vertex AI and then execute a
similarity search for related data within Cloud SQL. Same as before, you
write the query, and Data Connect does the rest. [CLICKING] Now that we've learned
about the technology that enabled us to
build the app, I'll hand it off to Michael
to dive into the code. MICHAEL BLEIGH: Thanks, Tyler. Data Connect starts
with a schema. Here, you can see my schema with
all the types for our movie app. As I update the schema
or add new fields, they'll be automatically
migrated in my local emulator environment and can be
deployed to production using the Firebase CLI. Once I have my
schema, Data Connect automatically generates a rich
GraphQL querying API for my app. Switching over to my queries,
I start by defining fragments. Fragments let me
fetch the same data in multiple places
in my queries, such as the basic movie
review information, including average review rating,
or the user information for a particular review. With Data Connect, I can
construct efficient queries that fetch all the data I
need in a single request. Even though our app's
Home page contains multiple distinct sections,
and each section contains nested relational
information, I can retrieve all of that data
using a single Home page query. I add a field for new
releases, for top-rated movies, and for recent reviews. I add the fragments to
get the data my UI needs, and I'm ready to go. But what about
something more complex like aggregations or
the semantic search we demoed earlier? Data Connect has a built-in
integration with Vertex AI to enable text
embedding directly from our GraphQL interface. For our movie search,
we compare an embedding of the user's query against the
already generated embeddings of the movie titles and
descriptions in our database. To pull in review statistics for
our movies, I used a view type. This special type allows me
to write arbitrary SQL SELECT
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
of the user's query against the
already generated embeddings of the movie titles and
descriptions in our database. To pull in review statistics for
our movies, I used a view type. This special type allows me
to write arbitrary SQL SELECT statements and bind
them to GraphQL data. View types can be integrated
into your data model relationally so that
when I query a movie, I can also pull its average
review rating, for instance. When you're ready to test
out any of your queries, you can do it
right from your IDE using the built-in query runner
that's part of the Data Connect VSCode extension. If you want a little help
writing your queries, you can even describe your
query in natural language and Gemini will help you write
it using the current schema for your specific application. Now that we've
built our query, how do we integrate it
into the application? Let's take a look at the
Browse Movie page, which has rich interactivity
as the user filters to find the movie they want. Data Connect brings the
end-to-end development experience together by
generating a strongly-typed SDK, based on your queries, that can
be used directly in your app. Using our new React
bindings, all I need to do is prepare the variables I want
to pass from the user interface and pass them to the React hook
that was automatically generated the moment I saved my query. As the variables
change from user input, the query is
automatically re-executed and the data is updated. I'm pretty happy with
the basics of our app, but there's a new feature
we've been working on that I'm even more excited
about, a movie recommendations agent that will pick the perfect
personalized movie for my users to watch. To create this agent, we'll
combine Data Connect's querying power with Gemini's intelligence
using the Genkit framework. Tyler, can you talk
a little about how the agent is going to work? [CLICKING] TYLER CROWE: Thanks, Michael. We've seen how easy it is to
build the backbone of an app with Data Connect. Let's take this
to the next level
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
Tyler, can you talk
a little about how the agent is going to work? [CLICKING] TYLER CROWE: Thanks, Michael. We've seen how easy it is to
build the backbone of an app with Data Connect. Let's take this
to the next level and create an agent that
can use tools to access data within our database. The first concept
we should cover, is Retrieval-Augmented
Generation, otherwise known as RAG. In a normal conversation,
a user will prompt an AI, which will give it an output. But if you want the AI to
give a relevant result based on data in your
database, you can use RAG to execute
a semantic search and pass the context to the AI. The AI response will now
contain the relevant data from your database. Let's add some more
complexity to this. You've created an agent that can
answer many types of questions. In order to answer
these questions, it will use a spec
from Data Connect that will tell it about the
available tools, queries. And mutations, it has to
answer the user's questions. It will choose the tool,
pass the right information from the prompt, and then return
an output based on the data it pulled from the right tool. So we want to build an
agent that can recommend the perfect movie to watch. In order to do that, we'll
create multiple tools that the agent has
at its disposal a way to search movies,
filter movies, search reviews, clarify a customer's question,
and finally, recommend movies. The agent may make multiple
calls to different tools to get the right information to
answer the question of the user. And of course, we don't
want to recommend a movie that the user has
already watched, so we'll RAG in the user's
past watch history and reviews. The general flow
goes as follows. The user enters a
prompt to the agent, and the past reviews and watch
history are provided as context to the agent. The agent then chooses
which tools to call and pulls data to try to answer
the question from the user. The user can then request
new recommendations or clarify their request. The agent then goes back
with the new information
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
The agent then chooses
which tools to call and pulls data to try to answer
the question from the user. The user can then request
new recommendations or clarify their request. The agent then goes back
with the new information and gathers additional data
using the tools it thinks are best to answer its question. When successful,
the user will accept the agent's recommendation. Michael, do you think we can
add this to our movie app? MICHAEL BLEIGH:
Yes we can, Tyler. Let's dive in. To set the scene,
let's take a quick look at the prompt that
will drive our agent. It has detailed guidance
to help the model use and find information to
recommend the perfect movie. It also has access
to a few tools already, ask question
and recommend movies. Tools allow an LLM to request
additional information, either from the user or from
data made available to it. It's what makes
an agent an agent. You can see at the
top, that there's a TODO, indicating that
we need to add tools that can talk to the database. Let's do that now. One of the central
concepts of Data Connect, is what we call a connector. You can think of a connector
as being a dedicated API for your application that's
built for a specific purpose. Earlier, we were editing
queries in the connector that was built for our web
app, but for our agent, we're going to create
a second connector. I've already created a
tools connector directory and added some queries
to it for my agent. Search movies to do
a semantic search, filter movies to find
movies by filtering based on various criteria,
and search reviews to search through user reviews. Data Connect supports generating
SDKs for JavaScript, Swift, Kotlin, and Dart, but it also
has one more feature, generating tool definitions for LLMs. In my connector.YAML, I'll
add an LLM tools entry and pick an output file. As soon as I Save, the
connector automatically generates the definitions file. Looking at the
generated tools JSON, you can see that it provides
information about my Data
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
In my connector.YAML, I'll
add an LLM tools entry and pick an output file. As soon as I Save, the
connector automatically generates the definitions file. Looking at the
generated tools JSON, you can see that it provides
information about my Data Connect service, as well
as JSON schema definitions and descriptions for each of
the queries that I defined. This provides a great basis
for working with any LLM's API, but we've made it even
easier for Genkit users. Genkit is an
open-source SDK to build JNI features server side in
JavaScript, Go and Python. Here, I'm integrating
it into my Next.js app and initializing it to
use the Gemini 2.0 Flash model by default, Genkit is extensible
through plugins, and the Firebase
plugin includes a way to add Data Connect
tools directly from the generated JSON file. I just need to add the
path to my generated file and pass a reference
to my Firebase App to configure the connection. Once I've done that, I can
switch over to the Genkit dev UI that lets me test
and play with everything plugged into my app. I see new tools are
available, db/SearchMovies, db/FilterMovies, and
db/SearchReviews. I can test these out and see
that it is returning data from my application. And now that these tools
are available to Genkit, I can add them to my prompt
and complete that TODO. So now my agent
can intelligently reason about when it
might need to search movies and reviews to find
a great movie to recommend. Let's take this new
agent for a spin. When I visit the Next
Watch feature of the app, I can see my Recent
Watch History with an open spot for what
the agent will recommend. I can pick from a few
preselected prompts, or I can ask it for something
I'm in the mood for. In this case, I'm going
to prompt for something I can watch with my daughter. Based on this prompt, the agent
will review my Watch History, including any
reviews I've written, to better understand
what to recommend. It can either go straight
to recommendations, or as we're seeing in this
case, ask a clarifying question.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – nZhkjSFB6UA
text:
Based on this prompt, the agent
will review my Watch History, including any
reviews I've written, to better understand
what to recommend. It can either go straight
to recommendations, or as we're seeing in this
case, ask a clarifying question. Here, it's asked me what
genre I want to watch? I'll say animation. A few seconds
later, the agent has made a recommendation of
three potential movies, and honestly, these all
look interesting to me. But my daughter has been really
interested in magic lately, so I'm going to refine
the recommendations by saying that she's
into magic and witches. The new set of
recommendations includes movies that incorporate all of
the information already provided but refines it further
based on the new request. With that, I can pick
the perfect movie to watch tonight
with my daughter. Data Connect speeds
up the process of building both applications
and agents by automatically generating the API
endpoints, SDKs, and tool definitions for my application. Tyler, can you give us a recap
of what we've done so far? [CLICKING] TYLER CROWE: Happy to, Michael. We started off by building a
connector on Data Connect that enables the web app to execute
queries and mutations that read and write to Cloud SQL
and execute semantic search with vector embeddings. We just created a new
connector that contains tools that our agent on Genkit can
call to power our movie agent experience. We encourage you to
try this out yourself. You can clone the agent and
movie app at the GitHub repo with the QR code,
and if you want to just start with the
app and semantic search, you can follow the step-by-step
instructions with our Codelab. Thank you and happy hacking. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
